> Can you create a turbopack counterpart?

This PR is intended to match what recently landed in the turbo repository: https://github.com/vercel/turbo/blob/827bea99b0972e69a2fcb68e0954315ffdfda942/Cargo.lock#L9479

## Failing test suites <!--24073899124-->
Commit: ad81977adffc97eda53f884c53c7acbb7985c21e

`TURBOPACK=1 pnpm test-start test/e2e/app-dir/navigation/navigation.test.ts` (turbopack)
- app dir - navigation > navigation between pages and app > should not continously initiate a mpa navigation to the same URL when router state changes

<details>
<summary>Expand output</summary>

  ● app dir - navigation › navigation between pages and app › should not continously initiate a mpa navigation to the same URL when router state changes

    thrown: "Exceeded timeout of 60000 ms for a test.
    Add a timeout value to this test to increase the timeout, if this is a long-running test. See https://jestjs.io/docs/api#testname-fn-timeout."

      643 |       // it triggers a hover on a link to initiate a prefetch request every second, and so we check that
      644 |       // it doesn't repeatedly initiate the mpa navigation request
    > 645 |       it('should not continously initiate a mpa navigation to the same URL when router state changes', async () => {
          |       ^
      646 |         let requestCount = 0
      647 |         await next.browser('/mpa-nav-test', {
      648 |           beforePageLoad(page) {

      at it (e2e/app-dir/navigation/navigation.test.ts:645:7)
      at describe (e2e/app-dir/navigation/navigation.test.ts:599:3)
      at Object.describe (e2e/app-dir/navigation/navigation.test.ts:5:1)

</details>

body: @xiaohanyu Claude Opus seems to think otherwise > Yes, changing "to" to "of" would be incorrect in this sentence. > > The correct preposition to use after "benefits" or "advantages" is "to," followed by a gerund (verb + -ing). This is because we're referring to the action or process of doing something. > > Correct: "There are a couple of benefits to doing the rendering work on the server, including:" > > Incorrect: "There are a couple of benefits of doing the rendering work on the server, including:" > > The phrase "benefits of" is typically followed by a noun or noun phrase, not a gerund. For example: "There are a couple of benefits of server-side rendering, including:" > > In this case, "server-side rendering" is a noun phrase, so "of" is the appropriate preposition.

body:
>`assetPrefix` is supposed to be a full domain

assetPrefix should also be able to work with a relative path afaik, as there are some checks somewhere to see if it's relative or not. Maybe unclear from the docs
author: Netail

body:
> > `assetPrefix` is supposed to be a full domain
> 
> assetPrefix should also be able to work with a relative path afaik, as there are some checks somewhere to see if it's relative or not. Maybe unclear from the docs

The docs state:
> Good to know: Next.js 9.5+ added support for a customizable [Base Path](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/next-config-js/basePath), which is better suited for hosting your application on a sub-path like /docs. We do not suggest you use a custom Asset Prefix for this use case.
author: edwinveldhuizen

body:
> > > `assetPrefix` is supposed to be a full domain
> > 
> > 
> > assetPrefix should also be able to work with a relative path afaik, as there are some checks somewhere to see if it's relative or not. Maybe unclear from the docs
> 
> The docs state:
> 
> > Good to know: Next.js 9.5+ added support for a customizable [Base Path](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/next-config-js/basePath), which is better suited for hosting your application on a sub-path like /docs. We do not suggest you use a custom Asset Prefix for this use case.

Mhhmmm, with "this use case" I think they mean all your page routes not your bundles.

With basePath you are not able to prefix only your bundles, e.g. having the routes
/winkels
/winkel/1234
with the bundle serving on `/stores-app/_next/...`

body:
~~This currently mixes two things, I think I'll split it up into two PRs~~
turns out that the fixes done here require both, so i think it makes more sense to keep it all in one
author: lubieowoce

body: Are all three bugs fixed with the same implementation change or can we land these separately?
author: eps1lon

body:
@eps1lon 

> Are all three bugs fixed with the same implementation change or can we land these separately?

Functionality-wise, they're all kind of related

Both of these are needed to fix the case when running on a platform with `waitUntil` but no `@{next,vercel}/request-context` (like `next-on-pages`)
- Fix: Make sure that calls to waitUntil that happen after adapter returns are awaited too 
- Fix: pass of waitUntil from server/web/adapter into edge SSR handlers

It won't work unless both changes are applied, so I feel like it's more useful to keep these atomic

This bit is only needed for the test that I added to verify the above:
- Fix: Call waitUntil for edge functions running in an EdgeRuntime sandbox 

I suppose I could split this bit out and land it first, but it's like 6 lines of code, so doesn't feel like it'd benefit readability. And without it, I can't add the test.
author: lubieowoce

Commit: 856b66313451f06738a16612907a14821c3ed6a4
author: ijjk

body:
>  As we enable new lint rules, they can be added as warnings which will gently nudge us towards fixing in related pull requests.

We can go with error instead (so that no new ones get introduced). When we do introduce new rules, we can codemod `eslint-disable` directives which makes it easier to grep for current violations. We can use [`eslint-focus`](https://github.com/eps1lon/eslint-focus#usage) for that: `npx eslint-focus <ruleOrRulePattern> <relativeOrAbsolutePaths..> --fix --fix-type add-disable-directive`
author: eps1lon

body:
> we can codemod eslint-disable directives

I'd argue that having many `disable` rules spread throughout the codebase won't actually help us improve the code over time. By allowing them to appear as warnings, it gives us feedback in the editor to fix the issues. If we add disable comments, there's no easy way for us to see that from within the editor while we're fixing other related code. The best way for us to incrementally adopt new rules would be for us to get gently nudged to fix the violations over time, not all at once. The disable comments hides the issue instead.
author: wyattjoh

body:
Thank you for raising this @karlkeefer, I've pushed a commit to rephrase a few things. 

@samcx maybe not supplying `generateStaticParams` should be the equivalent of supplying an empty array. But I'll defer that to your team :)
author: delbaoliveira

body:
> A possible discussion to have is whether revalidate should just enable it, without an empty generateStaticParams. :frog-eyes:

Or setting `export const dynamic = 'force-static'`. Having those options available but not actually making a route static, unless you add a `generateStaticParams` function that returns an empty array is the kind of DX that makes someone lose their mind.
author: rijk

body: @rijk Thanks for the feedback! Actually not 100% with `export const dynamic = 'force-static`—taking a look
author: samcx

body: @rijk It turns out you can use `force-static`! I'll be updating our again

  • Did you mean: **@types~~-~~/**react-dom (385 thousand times more downloads)

  • packages/next/package.json

[**View full report↗︎**](https://socket.dev/dashboard/org/vercel/diff/7b459381-f8a2-4030-a190-7997db3c0cb8/312ebbcc-9556-4e5f-925a-a75d25f26db2) ### Next steps
What is a typosquat?

Package name is similar to other popular packages and may not be the package you want.

Use care when consuming similarly named packages and ensure that you did not intend to consume a different package. Malicious packages often publish using similar names as existing popular packages.

Take a deeper look at the dependency

Take a moment to review the security alert above. Review the linked package source code to understand the potential risk. Ensure the package is not malicious before proceeding. If you're unsure how to proceed, reach out to your security team or ask the Socket team for help at support [AT] socket [DOT] dev.

Remove the package

If you happen to install a dependency that Socket reports as Known Malware you should immediately remove it and select a different dependency. For other alert types, you may may wish to investigate alternative packages or consider if there are other ways to mitigate the specific risk posed by the dependency.

Mark a package as acceptable risk

To ignore an alert, reply with a comment starting with @SocketSecurity ignore followed by a space separated list of ecosystem/package-name@version specifiers. e.g. @SocketSecurity ignore npm/foo@1.0.0 or ignore all packages with @SocketSecurity ignore-all

  • @SocketSecurity ignore npm/types-react-dom@19.0.0-rc.0

## Failing test suites <!--24170026993-->
Commit: 6a400dd49d082796492e9960a5970ff8f203825b

`TURBOPACK=1 pnpm test-start test/e2e/app-dir/app-static/app-static-custom-handler.test.ts` (turbopack)
- app-dir static/dynamic handling > should have deterministic etag across revalidates

<details>
<summary>Expand output</summary>

    Failing test passed even though it was supposed to fail. Remove `.failing` to remove error.

    > 623 |     it.failing(

      626 |         const initialRes = await next.fetch(

      at Object.require (e2e/app-dir/app-static/app-static-custom-handler.test.ts:2:1)

</details>

## Failing test suites <!--24366759829-->
Commit: 9c8c659158c294ac7e2433850968f7a5ee3fd4c9

`pnpm test-dev test/development/middleware-errors/index.test.ts`
- middleware - development errors > when there is a compilation error after boot > logs the error correctly

<details>
<summary>Expand output</summary>

  ● middleware - development errors › when there is a compilation error after boot › logs the error correctly

    TIMED OUT: success

    undefined

    Error: expect(received).toEqual(expected) // deep equality

    Expected: 2
    Received: 3

      698 |
      699 |   if (hardError) {
    > 700 |     throw new Error('TIMED OUT: ' + regex + '\n\n' + content + '\n\n' + lastErr)
          |           ^
      701 |   }
      702 |   return false
      703 | }

      at check (lib/next-test-utils.ts:700:11)
      at Object.<anonymous> (development/middleware-errors/index.test.ts:289:7)

</details>


Read more about building and testing Next.js in [contributing.md](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/contributing.md).
author: ijjk

body: What's the benefit over doing this with ESLint? I remember there were some distinction but I'm not sure if `noUnusedLocals` or ESLint had more false-positives.
author: eps1lon

body:
Thanks for bringing this up. For the typescript files, this is indeed already covered by the eslint rules (via `@typescript-eslint/no-unused-vars`). This rule is not applied for `"test/**/*.js"` though, which is why the aforementioned dead code slipped through. Since, as mentioned in the PR description, I do not intend to enable `checkJs`, the compiler option `noUnusedLocals` won't give us any benefit over the current setup, so I'm closing this PR.

EDIT: Actually, the test js files are also covered, but these specific files (`test/integration/eslint/**`) are listed in `.eslintignore`.
author: unstubbable

## Failing test suites <!--24186237821-->
Commit: 44cb605945430dd012ce2cf78a0f50227cbc3916

`TURBOPACK=1 pnpm test-dev test/e2e/prerender.test.ts` (turbopack)
- Prerender > should log error in console and browser in development mode

<details>
<summary>Expand output</summary>

  ● Prerender › should log error in console and browser in development mode

    page.waitForSelector: Timeout 60000ms exceeded.
    Call log:
      - waiting for locator('#after-change')

      421 |     return this.chain(() => {
      422 |       return page
    > 423 |         .waitForSelector(selector, { timeout, state: 'attached' })
          |          ^
      424 |         .then(async (el) => {
      425 |           // it seems selenium waits longer and tests rely on this behavior
      426 |           // so we wait for the load event fire before returning

      at waitForSelector (lib/browsers/playwright.ts:423:10)

</details>


Read more about building and testing Next.js in [contributing.md](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/contributing.md).

## Tests Passed

author: ijjk

body: Putting this back on hold, for some reason the fix does not work anymore. Might revisit later.
author: unstubbable

body: The observation that the fix suddenly didn't work anymore was grounded in the fact that I originally tested with an app that was using the edge runtime (which worked), and now tested with an app that is using the node runtime (which did not work). In the latter case, due to the bundled server runtimes being used, the original names can not be mapped correctly. The only solution I found for this was to build the Next.js sources with `eval-source-map` instead. That not only fixes the issue, but also allows us to get rid of the workaround of not calling `preloadEntries` when debugging.
author: unstubbable

body: What's the downside of increasing the ID space?
author: eps1lon

body:
> What's the downside of increasing the ID space?

This will increase the bundles by a few bytes because module IDs will be longer. I'm hoping it will be shown in the PR stats.
author: unstubbable

body:
## Failing test suites <!--24499333908-->
Commit: eb8cc9ae3f03865421be84af507119fdaba93b00

`__NEXT_EXPERIMENTAL_PPR=true pnpm test-dev test/e2e/app-dir/navigation/navigation.test.ts` (PPR)
- app dir - navigation > browser back to a revalidated page > should load the page

<details>
<summary>Expand output</summary>

  ● app dir - navigation › browser back to a revalidated page › should load the page

    page.waitForSelector: Timeout 60000ms exceeded.
    Call log:
      - waiting for locator('#submit-button')

      419 |     return this.chain(() => {
      420 |       return page
    > 421 |         .waitForSelector(selector, { timeout, state: 'attached' })
          |          ^
      422 |         .then(async (el) => {
      423 |           // it seems selenium waits longer and tests rely on this behavior
      424 |           // so we wait for the load event fire before returning

      at waitForSelector (lib/browsers/playwright.ts:421:10)

</details>


Read more about building and testing Next.js in [contributing.md](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/contributing.md).
author: ijjk

body:
In the CI, and sometimes also locally, a few of the test apps fail to build due to module id conflicts.

```
Error: Assigning deterministic module ids has lead to 1 conflict.
  Increase the 'maxLength' to increase the id space and make conflicts less likely
  (recommended when there are many conflicts or application is expected to grow),
  or add an 'salt' number to try another hash starting value in the same id space
  (recommended when there is only a single conflict).
```

Increasing the ID space size from 6 to 7 or defining a `salt` to fix those builds now starts to feel like black magic to me.
@sokra As the author of the `DeterministicModuleIdsPlugin`, may I ask you to add your opinion on this whole approach?

author: ijjk

body:
> Increasing the ID space size from 6 to 7 or defining a `salt` to fix those builds now starts to feel like black magic to me. @sokra As the author of the `DeterministicModuleIdsPlugin`, may I ask you to add your opinion on this whole approach?

After looking a bit closer at [`assignDeterministicIds`](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/400db90db807d630b6c9f5e7ccde2b8f01690f3b/lib/ids/IdHelpers.js#L376) and  [`numberHash`](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/400db90db807d630b6c9f5e7ccde2b8f01690f3b/lib/util/numberHash.js#L96), I don't find it surprising anymore that we had the frequent ID collisions, and that a `maxLength` of 8, which I've now settled on, would be needed to reduce the collision probability close to zero. I assume the default of 3 is a tradeoff that's more targeted towards client bundles where the bundle size is even more important.

The remaining test failures are unrelated flakes, so I'm setting this to "ready for review".
author: unstubbable

body:
Increasing `maxLength` manually is not necessary. Webpack automatically chooses a `maxLength` depending on the number of IDs (using 5% fill rate of the id space). (https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/400db90db807d630b6c9f5e7ccde2b8f01690f3b/lib/ids/IdHelpers.js#L401)
On conflicts it automatically computes a new ID until it found a fitting ID. (https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/400db90db807d630b6c9f5e7ccde2b8f01690f3b/lib/ids/IdHelpers.js#L411-L413)
The conflict avoidance it deterministic, so you even get deterministic IDs even in case of conflicts. (https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/400db90db807d630b6c9f5e7ccde2b8f01690f3b/lib/ids/IdHelpers.js#L386)
Actually conflicts are quite common, as we use a very small hash size to generate small bundles. (https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/400db90db807d630b6c9f5e7ccde2b8f01690f3b/lib/ids/IdHelpers.js#L388-L392)
Larger hash/number sizes have bad effects on the bundle size, especially on the gzipped bundle size, since they have a high entropy.

So I don't think that PR makes sense and I also think flakes are not caused by ID assignment. Different IDs might be caused by different identifiers that are hashed.
author: sokra

body:
Thanks for pointing out those details, that makes totally sense now, especially that the conflict avoidance is also deterministic, **if** the module request URIs are deterministic. I'll have look whether I can find a reason for why they currently might not be.

EDIT: It's still interesting though that changing the `maxLength` does fix `deterministic build > should have same md5 file across build` according to my testing. 🤔

body: @KentoMoriwaki could you elaborate on the global.css change, are you using Turbopack or webpack?
author: timneutkens

body:
@timneutkens Thanks for your quick response!

I have created a repository to reproduce the issue:
https://github.com/KentoMoriwaki/next-tailwind

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Run `pnpm i` to install dependencies.
2. Run `pnpm run dev` to start the development server.
3. Access `localhost:3000/pages-router`.
4. Edit `src/app/globals.css` (e.g., change the margin of the body on line 21).
5. Confirm that the page reloads.

When running `pnpm run dev --turbo`, the issue does not occur. Occasionally, due to cache effects or other factors, the reload might happen or not happen. In such cases, restarting the dev server should result in the expected behavior.

Additionally, changes to parts of `app/layout.tsx` also cause reloads (e.g., changing the `lang` attribute of the `html` tag), indicating that the issue is not confined to just CSS changes.
author: KentoMoriwaki

body: This issue seems to be related: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/64743
author: KentoMoriwaki

body:
I'm having this issue as well and the fix seems to work for me. The setup in our project is the same, with the full page reload on routes in `pages` being traced back to importing `globals.css` in `app/layout.tsx`.

To replicate:
- Create a new next project using `npx create-next-app@latest hmr-test`
- Do not use app router
- Once the project is set up, create `src/app/something/page.tsx`, auto generate `src/app/layout.tsx`, and add `import '../styles/global.css'` to `src/app/layout.tsx`

Now, every time you make a change to a page route, HMR will reload the whole page. Switching to turbopack doesn't seem to make a difference
author: adamth
